1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}
67	
68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	\leanok
96	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
97	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
98	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
99	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
100	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
101	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
102	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
103	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
104	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
105	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
106	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
107	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
108	\end{proof}
109	
110	\begin{lemma}\leanok
111	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
112	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
114	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
115	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
116	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
117	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
118	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
119	  isomorphism.
120	\end{lemma}
121	
122	\begin{proof}
123	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
124	  \leanok
125	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
126	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
127	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
128	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
129	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
130	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
131	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
132	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
133	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
134	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
135	\end{proof}
136	
137	\begin{lemma}\leanok
138	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
139	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
140	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
141	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
142	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
143	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
144	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
145	\end{lemma}
146	
147	\begin{proof}
148	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
149	  \leanok
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative.
162	
163	\begin{lemma}\leanok
164	[Affine base change]
165	  \label{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
166	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}
167	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
168	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
169	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I)
170	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L906--918).
171	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
172	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
173	  % Assume $f$ is affine.
174	  % In this case $f_*\mathcal{F} \cong Rf_*\mathcal{F}$ is
175	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf, and for every base change diagram
176	  % (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
177	  % we have
178	  % $$
179	  % g^*f_*\mathcal{F} = f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}.
180	  % $$"
181	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
182	  change''.}
183	  Assume \(f : X \to S\) is an affine morphism and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
184	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then for every base-change square as
185	  above the canonical map of
186	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
187	  \[
188	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\cong\; f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr).
189	  \]
190	\end{lemma}
191	
192	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The vanishing of higher direct images is
193	% Lemma \ref{lemma-relative-affine-vanishing}.
194	% The statement is local on $S$ and $S'$. Hence we may
195	% assume $X = \Spec(A)$, $S = \Spec(R)$,
196	% $S' = \Spec(R')$ and $\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}$
197	% for some $A$-module $M$.
198	% We use Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback}
199	% to describe pullbacks and pushforwards of $\mathcal{F}$.
200	% Namely, $X' = \Spec(R' \otimes_R A)$ and
201	% $\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated
202	% to $(R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M$.
203	% Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
204	% equality
205	% $$
206	% (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
207	% $$
208	% as $R'$-modules."
209	% NOTE: Lean proof incomplete (sorry at the section-iso step). The
210	% `Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app` reduction lands, but closing the per-section goal
211	% needs a Mathlib gap NOT yet present: the affine dictionary translating
212	% Scheme.Modules.pushforward/pullback of tilde-modules on Spec into
213	% restrictScalars / base change, plus an "iso local on an affine cover"
214	% criterion for SheafOfModules maps. The closing algebra
215	% (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange) exists in Mathlib. See
216	% informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
217	\begin{proof}
218	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
219	  \emph{First reduction (locality).} By
220	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
221	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
222	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\); over such an affine open
223	  the tilde dictionary below applies and computes the section map explicitly. Fix
224	  one such affine open.
225	
226	  The assertion is local on \(S\) and on \(S'\), so we may assume all schemes in
227	  sight are affine: \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
228	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), with \(f\) corresponding to a ring map
229	  \(R \to A\) and \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) the quasi-coherent sheaf
230	  associated to an \(A\)-module \(M\). Computing the fibre product gives
231	  \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), and the pullback
232	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to
233	  the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). Under the
234	  dictionary between quasi-coherent sheaves on an affine scheme and modules,
235	  pushforward along an affine morphism is restriction of scalars and pullback
236	  along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_R R'\). The base-change map of
237	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is therefore identified with
238	  the canonical \(R'\)-linear comparison map for the two ways of forming the
239	  iterated tensor product, and the claim reduces to the associativity isomorphism
240	  \[
241	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;=\; R' \otimes_R M
242	  \]
243	  of \(R'\)-modules, which is an isomorphism (no flatness is needed in the affine
244	  case).
245	\end{proof}
246	
247	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
248	
249	\begin{theorem}\leanok
250	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
251	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
253	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
254	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
255	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
256	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
257	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
258	  % $$
259	  % \xymatrix{
260	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
261	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
262	  % }
263	  % $$
264	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
265	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
266	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
267	  % For any $i \geq 0$
268	  % \begin{enumerate}
269	  % \item the base change map of
270	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
271	  % is an isomorphism
272	  % $$
273	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
274	  % $$
275	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
276	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
277	  % \end{enumerate}"
278	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
279	  \(i = 0\) case.}
280	  Consider the cartesian square
281	  \[
282	    \begin{array}{ccc}
283	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
284	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
285	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
286	    \end{array}
287	  \]
288	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
289	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
290	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
291	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
292	  \[
293	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
294	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
295	  \]
296	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
297	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
298	  \[
299	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
300	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
301	  \]
302	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
303	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
304	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
305	\end{theorem}
306	
307	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Having said this, part (1) follows from part (2). Namely,
308	% part (1) is local on $S'$ and hence we may assume $S$
309	% and $S'$ are affine. In other words, we have $S = \Spec(A)$
310	% and $S' = \Spec(B)$ as in (2).
311	% Then since $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent
312	% (Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images}),
313	% it is the quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_S$-module associated to the
314	% $A$-module $H^0(S, R^if_*\mathcal{F}) = H^i(X, \mathcal{F})$
315	% (equality by
316	% Lemma \ref{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}).
317	% Similarly, $R^if'_*\mathcal{F}'$ is the quasi-coherent
318	% $\mathcal{O}_{S'}$-module associated to the $B$-module
319	% $H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$. Since pullback by $g$ corresponds
320	% to $- \otimes_A B$ on modules
321	% (Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback})
322	% we see that it suffices to prove (2).
323	%
324	% Let $A \to B$ be a flat ring homomorphism.
325	% Let $X$ be a quasi-compact and quasi-separated scheme over $A$.
326	% Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module.
327	% Set $X_B = X \times_{\Spec(A)} \Spec(B)$ and denote
328	% $\mathcal{F}_B$ the pullback of $\mathcal{F}$.
329	% We are trying to show that the map
330	% $$
331	% H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^i(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
332	% $$
333	% (given by the reference in the statement of the lemma)
334	% is an isomorphism.
335	%
336	% In case $X$ is separated, choose an affine open covering
337	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$ and recall that
338	% $$
339	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F}),
340	% $$
341	% see
342	% Lemma \ref{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent}.
343	% If $\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \ldots \cup (U_t)_B$ we obtain
344	% by base change, then it is still the case that each $(U_i)_B$ is affine
345	% and that $X_B$ is separated. Thus we obtain
346	% $$
347	% \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
348	% $$
349	% We have the following relation between the {\v C}ech complexes
350	% $$
351	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
352	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
353	% $$
354	% as follows from
355	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
356	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology."
357	% NOTE: Lean proof is a documented sorry (deferred). Depends on the affine
358	% lemma above PLUS Čech-cohomology / affine-cover infrastructure for
359	% SheafOfModules, which Mathlib does not provide. This is a multi-lane engine
360	% build, not a single-iter target. See informal/affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso.md.
361	\begin{proof}
362	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
363	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
364	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
365	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
366	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
367	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
368	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
369	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
370	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}')\). Since pullback along \(g\) corresponds to
371	  \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the comparison map of
372	  \(B\)-modules
373	  \[
374	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
375	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
376	  \]
377	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
378	
379	  \medskip
380	  \emph{The separated case via {\v C}ech complexes.} Suppose first that \(X\) is
381	  separated over \(A\). Choose a finite affine open cover
382	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\); by separatedness all finite
383	  intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are again affine, so {\v C}ech cohomology
384	  computes sheaf cohomology:
385	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^p(X, \mathcal{F})\). Base changing
386	  the cover yields an affine open cover
387	  \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of the again-separated
388	  \(X_B\), with the same property
389	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) = H^p(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
390	  affine case (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) identifies each
391	  term of the base-changed {\v C}ech complex with the original term tensored over
392	  \(A\) with \(B\); that is, there is an isomorphism of cochain complexes
393	  \[
394	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) \;\cong\;
395	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B .
396	  \]
397	  Because \(A \to B\) is flat, \(- \otimes_A B\) is an exact functor and hence
398	  commutes with the formation of cohomology of a complex; taking \(H^0\)
399	  (degree-zero cohomology) gives the desired isomorphism
400	  \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\).
401	
402	  \medskip
403	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated.
404	  Choose a finite affine open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\).
405	  Now the intersections \(U_{i_0 \dots i_p}\) are quasi-compact and separated
406	  rather than affine, but they fall under the separated case already treated, so
407	  flat base change holds for each of them. One then compares the two
408	  {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequences with \(E_2\)-pages
409	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}))\) and
410	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B))\), converging to
411	  \(H^{p+q}(X, \mathcal{F})\) and \(H^{p+q}(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\) respectively.
412	  The separated case provides the term-by-term identification
413	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}_B, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F}_B)) =
414	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \underline{H}^q(\mathcal{F})) \otimes_A B\) on the
415	  \(E_2\)-pages, and flatness of \(A \to B\) propagates this isomorphism through
416	  the spectral sequences to the abutment. In particular, in degree \(0\) one
417	  obtains \(H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as
418	  required. (For the \(i = 0\) statement at hand, one may avoid the spectral
419	  sequence entirely and argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of
420	  cover members, reducing each step to the separated case.)
421	\end{proof}
422

# Blueprint-writer directive — iter-235 — FlatBaseChange affine reframe + the single Mathlib-absent brick

## Chapter
`blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`

## Context (self-contained)
A mathlib-analogist consult (verified against current Mathlib; persistent rationale in
`analogies/fbc-dict.md` — read it) found that the affine reduction of this chapter was framed at the
wrong altitude and that the Lean statements were missing a hypothesis. Three corrections must be reflected
in the prose. Do NOT add/remove any `\leanok`/`\mathlibok` markers (sync_leanok/review own those).

### Correction 1 — the statements require quasi-coherence (soundness)
Both `lem:affine_base_change_pushforward` (`\lean{…affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso}`) and
`thm:flat_base_change_pushforward` (`\lean{…flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}`) are about a
quasi-coherent `\(\mathcal F\)` (the chapter title already says so), but the Lean signatures took an
arbitrary `F : X.Modules`. The Lean decls are being amended THIS iter (refactor) to carry
`[F.IsQuasicoherent]` (Mathlib typeclass `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent`; `tilde M` carries the
instance). Update each statement block's prose to state explicitly that `\(\mathcal F\)` is assumed
quasi-coherent and that this hypothesis is essential (over an affine open a general module is not
`\(\widetilde M\)`, so the affine computation needs it). Keep the existing verbatim Stacks
`% SOURCE QUOTE` blocks byte-for-byte intact — they already say "quasi-coherent".

### Correction 2 — reframe the affine lemma's proof around `tilde` full-faithfulness
Replace the affine lemma's proof reframe (and the stale `% NOTE:` at ~lines 209–216 describing a
"missing affine dictionary … Mathlib gap") with the consult's correct reduction. The corrected sketch:
- After the locality first reduction (`lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens`) and localizing to
  `\(S=\Spec R,\ S'=\Spec R',\ X=\Spec A,\ \mathcal F=\widetilde M\)`, the base-change map is a morphism
  `\(\alpha\)` of quasi-coherent `\((\Spec R')\)`-modules.
- `\(\mathrm{tilde}\)` (the functor `\(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\,R' \to (\Spec R').\mathrm{Modules}\)`) is
  FULLY FAITHFUL with essential image the quasi-coherent modules, and its counit
  `\(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\)` is an isomorphism exactly on quasi-coherent modules
  (`isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff`). Hence for `\(\alpha\)` between quasi-coherent modules, counit naturality
  (`fromTildeΓNatTrans`) gives `\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha \iff \mathrm{IsIso}(\mathrm{moduleSpec}\Gamma(\alpha))\)`,
  reducing the goal to `\(\mathrm{IsIso}\)` of a CONCRETE `\(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\,R'\)` map.
- That concrete map is the `\(R'\)`-linear base-change comparison, and the claim closes by the
  associativity / cancellation isomorphism `\((R'\otimes_R A)\otimes_A M \cong R'\otimes_R M\)`
  (`TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange`, present in Mathlib, no flatness).
- State that this reframe avoids ALL section-level `\(\mathrm{SMul}\)`/`\(\mathrm{Module}\)`-instance
  plumbing (the iter-234 instance wall was a wrong-altitude symptom). As construction hints (in prose,
  no Lean tactics): the ring map underlying the pushforward at `\(\top\)` is `\(f.\mathrm{appTop}\)`
  (conjugate to `\(\varphi\)` for `\(f=\Spec.map\,\varphi\)` by `ΓSpecIso_inv_naturality`);
  `\((\Spec.map\,\varphi)^{-1}\top=\top\)` holds definitionally (`Scheme.preimage_top`); and any residual
  `\(\mathrm{ModuleCat}\)`-level scalar identity is discharged by materialising the action with
  `\(\mathrm{Module.compHom}\)`/`\(\mathrm{IsScalarTower}\)` as Mathlib's own `Tilde.lean` does.

### Correction 3 — add the single Mathlib-absent brick as a named lemma
Add ONE new lemma block (in the affine section, before `lem:affine_base_change_pushforward`) stating the
genuinely Mathlib-absent ingredient the lane reduces to:
- `\label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}` (NO `\lean{}` pin — the Lean decl is not yet built; this is the
  next FlatBaseChange prover target, to be pinned once it lands).
- `\uses{}` as appropriate (it is bespoke infrastructure; no external source proof — it is the
  affine-pushforward-of-tilde formula). You MAY cite Stacks "schemes-lemma-widetilde-pullback" as the
  classical source if you retrieve and quote it; otherwise mark it Archon-original infrastructure with no
  `% SOURCE QUOTE` (do NOT fabricate a quote).
- Statement: for a ring map `\(\varphi : R \to R'\)` and an `\(R'\)`-module `\(M\)`, there is a canonical
  isomorphism of `\((\Spec R)\)`-modules
  `\(\mathrm{pushforward}(\Spec.map\,\varphi)(\widetilde M) \cong \widetilde{(\mathrm{restrictScalars}\,\varphi)\,M}\)`.
- Note in the block that this single iso discharges BOTH (a) the global-sections / Γ-fragment comparison
  (via `\(\mathrm{moduleSpec}\Gamma\)` of the iso) AND (b) quasi-coherence of the pushforward of a tilde
  module (via closed-under-iso of the QC predicate + `\((\widetilde N)\) is quasi-coherent`), so no
  separate "pushforward preserves quasi-coherent" theorem is needed.

### Correction 4 — refresh the flat theorem's stale NOTE
The `% NOTE:` at ~lines 357–360 on `thm:flat_base_change_pushforward` may stay (the Čech/affine-cover
infrastructure for SheafOfModules IS still genuinely Mathlib-absent and the theorem IS a multi-lane build),
but update its wording so it points at `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` + the reframe as the affine input,
not the superseded "affine dictionary" framing.

## Out of scope
- Do NOT touch the `def:pushforward_base_change_map` block or the 3 locality lemmas
  (`lem:modules_isIso_*`) — they are correct and closed.
- Do NOT alter the verbatim Stacks `% SOURCE QUOTE` / `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF` text.
- No Lean tactic syntax in prose.

## mathlib-analogist `fbc-dict` findings (the STUCK corrective paid off — high value)

The consult both dissolved the instance wall AND surfaced a **critical soundness defect**:

1. **CRITICAL (acted on this iter): missing `[F.IsQuasicoherent]`.** Both
   `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` and `flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` took an arbitrary
   `F : X.Modules` — FALSE as typed (Stacks 02KH requires QC; over an affine open a general F is not
   `tilde M`, so the affine proof cannot apply). Verified `SheafOfModules.IsQuasicoherent` is the
   Mathlib typeclass (loogle; `tilde M` carries the instance) and no downstream consumer exists (grep).
   **Fixed this iter** via refactor `fbc-qc-hyp` (added `[F.IsQuasicoherent]` to both; build green, 2
   pre-existing sorries, no new binders). This is the "soundness check before spending budget" rule
   firing for real: the lane's recurring blocker was partly a *false statement*, not only a Mathlib gap.
2. **The instance wall was a wrong-altitude symptom.** `tilde.functor` is fully faithful with essential
   image QC; for QC modules `IsIso α ↔ IsIso (moduleSpecΓFunctor.map α)` via the `fromTildeΓ` counit,
   reducing the goal to `IsIso` of a concrete `ModuleCat R` map (`cancelBaseChange`) with NO section-smul
   instances. (And the iter-234 "action not synthesizable" claim was refuted — Mathlib materialises it
   with `letI`/`haveI`.)
3. **The lane reduces to ONE Mathlib-absent brick**, not a multi-brick chain:
   `pushforward (Spec.map φ)(tilde M) ≅ tilde (restrictScalars φ.hom M)`. This single iso discharges BOTH
   the Γ-fragment (Q1) AND QC-of-pushforward (Q2, which is otherwise genuinely Mathlib-absent but needs
   no separate theorem). Idiom corrections: ring-map accessor is `.hom`/`f.appTop` (not `.val`);
   `(Spec.map φ)⁻¹ᵁ ⊤ = ⊤` is `rfl` (`Scheme.preimage_top`). Rationale persisted in `analogies/fbc-dict.md`.

**FlatBaseChange is therefore NOT a hopeless grind** — STUCK was the right diagnosis and the consult
converted it into a bounded, sound next step. This iter: soundness fixed (refactor) + chapter reframed
(blueprint-writer `fbc-reframe` + blueprint-clean). NEXT FlatBaseChange prover round (next iter,
mathlib-build): build `lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso`, then close the affine lemma via the
full-faithfulness reframe + `cancelBaseChange`. The instance-wall route is abandoned.

## Rebuttal status

slot replaced by the consult, which then drove this iter's soundness fix + chapter reframe).
